children.value: This chapter documents the analysis pipeline for characterising the latent dimensions of inter-individual variation in behaviour. The approach takes the per-participant random-effect estimates (Best Linear Unbiased Predictors, BLUPs) from
and submits them to a factor analysis after standardisation and redundancy pruning.
Each participant produces a vector of behavioural traits via the random-effect BLUPs from M1, M3, M4, M5, M6, and M7. These traits include things like overall accuracy (
), and so on. The hypothesis is that these traits are driven by a small number of underlying trait-level dimensions. The factor analysis aims to:
Recover these dimensions empirically from the BLUP matrix.
Provide per-participant scores on each dimension.

Enable later cross-validation against the BayesFlow parameter posteriors fit to the same trials. If the GLMMs and the generative model are capturing the same individual differences, the same low-dimensional structure should emerge from both.
Step 1: BLUP collection and feature selection

children.label: Step 1: BLUP collection and feature selection

children.value: Each model that successfully fits with random slopes writes a
file with one row per participant and one column per random-effect term. The factor structure script discovers and loads all such files in the input directory.

A theoretically-motivated subset of BLUPs is retained (not all random effects are included because this would dilute the factor structure with measurement noise); including only those with named theoretical interpretations preserves the signal. The current inclusion list is:
Models that fall to OLS or cluster-robust OLS (and therefore have no extractable random effects, see
) contribute NaN columns; these are dropped before standardisation.
Each retained BLUP column is z-scored across participants:

children.html: z~p,k=bp,k−bˉ⋅,kSD(b⋅,k).\tilde z_{p,k} = \frac{b_{p,k} - \bar{b}_{\cdot,k}}{\mathrm{SD}(b_{\cdot,k})}.z~p,k​=SD(b⋅,k​)bp,k​−bˉ⋅,k​​.

children.value: This is essential because BLUPs from different models live on different scales (M5 is in degrees, M4 is on a log scale, M7 is in logit units). Without z-scoring, M5 would dominate any unrotated factor solution purely because of its scale.

children.html: ∣r∣>0.95\lvert r \rvert > 0.95∣r∣>0.95

children.value: . When such pairs are detected, the lower-priority member of each pair is dropped, where the priority order is fixed to preserve features mapping onto the focal theoretical constructs:
The full priority list appears in
. The motivation for this step is the following: the M4 random-effect covariance (
) is typically near-singular at small N because the three components share much of the same participant-level variance. The corresponding BLUPs are then near-perfectly correlated (e.g.,

children.html: ∣r∣>0.99\lvert r \rvert > 0.99∣r∣>0.99

children.value: in pilot data), which makes the correlation matrix singular, breaks KMO and Bartlett, and destabilises the factor solution. Auto-pruning preserves the factor most directly tied to the focal hypothesis (

). Thus this approach allows to preserve methodological rigor, regardless of the sample size (and it also allows for a more nuanced and sophisticated analysis with the same code as soon as more data is available).
Three diagnostics are computed and reported:
A near-zero determinant indicates remaining collinearity even after the explicit pruning step. We report

children.html: det⁡(R)\det(\mathbf{R})det(R)

children.value: in the diagnostics file as a transparent indicator.

Step 5: Factor count via parallel analysis

children.label: Step 5: Factor count via parallel analysis

children.value: ) to determine the number of factors. The procedure:
Compute the eigenvalues of the observed correlation matrix.
random matrices of the same shape with i.i.d. standard-normal entries.
Compute the eigenvalues of each random matrix and take the per-rank 95th percentile across iterations.

children.value: We decided to use PAF for this factor analysis pipeline, since it is the standard fitting method when distributional normality of the input is questionable (Fabrigar, Wegener, MacCallum, & Strahan, 1999) and works well with the standardised but possibly non-Gaussian BLUPs.

children.html: γ=0\gamma = 0γ=0

children.value: ). Oblique (rather than orthogonal) rotation is appropriate when there is no theoretical reason to assume the underlying factors are uncorrelated (and in this case, the M4 random-effect covariance structure already shows that they are not).
The rotation is implemented via the gradient-projection algorithm (
on the projected gradient norm; backtracking line search (Armijo condition) controls the step size. Maximum 500 outer iterations.

Why we implemented the factor analysis “from scratch”

children.label: Why we implemented the factor analysis “from scratch”

children.value: The factor analysis is implemented in-house (numpy + scipy only) rather than using the
Python package. The decision was forced by a known compatibility break between

). Rather than pinning to outdated dependencies (which could lead to compatibility issues with the dependency requirements for the other analyses), we implement PAF, oblimin rotation, KMO, and Bartlett directly. This also makes the framework self-contained and easier to audit.
Per-participant factor scores are computed via

children.html: kkk

children.value: These scores are intended to support downstream analyses, including correlation with BayesFlow parameter posterior means, with state and trait questionnaire scores, and with the change-point and survival analysis outputs. Most notably, the factor scores could be used for cross-validation of the BayesFlow pipeline and the respective estimated parameters.
To quantify uncertainty in the loadings (at the currently small-medium N typical of this study), we bootstrap the loading matrix:

(The sign-alignment step is necessary because factor analysis is identified up to sign and column permutation; without alignment, the bootstrap mean and SD are inflated by sign flips that have no substantive meaning.)

Per-participant factor scores at the recommended

children.html: kkk

children.value: is supplied, the script additionally produces

. This is a heatmap of correlations between the factor scores and a small set of model-free per-participant summary statistics (mean angular error, mean circular variance, mean defense rate, mean placement duration, mean

children.html: Δt\Delta_tΔt​

children.value: ). The idea is, that each factor should correlate with theoretically related summaries; if it does not, the factor labelling is unreliable (or it captures another latent structure that might not be meaningful and interpretable in our context).

children.alt: The current factor structure, based on piloting data only, thus interpret with caution!

Helpful tutorial papers for Exploratory Factor Analysis

children.label: Helpful tutorial papers for Exploratory Factor Analysis

children.value: Costello, A. B., & Osborne, J. W. (2005). Best practices in exploratory factor analysis: Four recommendations for getting the most from your analysis.
Fabrigar, L. R., Wegener, D. T., MacCallum, R. C., & Strahan, E. J. (1999). Evaluating the use of exploratory factor analysis in psychological research.
Bartlett, M. S. (1951). The effect of standardization on a chi-square approximation in factor analysis.
Carroll, J. B. (1953). An analytical solution for approximating simple structure in factor analysis.
Fabrigar, L. R., Wegener, D. T., MacCallum, R. C., & Strahan, E. J. (1999). Evaluating the use of exploratory factor analysis in psychological research.
Horn, J. L. (1965). A rationale and test for the number of factors in factor analysis.
The multivariate social scientist: Introductory statistics using generalized linear models
Jennrich, R. I. (2002). A simple general method for oblique rotation.
Kaiser, H. F. (1974). An index of factorial simplicity.
Zwick, W. R., & Velicer, W. F. (1986). Comparison of five rules for determining the number of components to retain.
